author: @adrianoVisoccini
text: Such things happen because big companies usually have a full law department where people mostly sit without a real job. And those people, they are always fear the company would hire a law company instead of their department, so they think they have to prove their usefulness by doing everything possible. We were having those problems being a contractor to biggest companies in our country. It doesn't matter how good your contact is written, even if it says "if anything happens we would pay for everything ourselves x2 without a court", they would "find"  something to correct there, not because it's significant, but because they think the SHOULD MAKE IT LOOK LIKE they are doing something

author: @shinrailp1416
text: Copyright strikes should be done the exact opposite way. The one claiming something should be the one to give detailed information on what is supposedly used and when it happens in a video. After that an actual person should be responsible for manually checking if the claim is valid and only THEN should the video be taken down. Not only would the added effort dissuade a lot of people form blindly abusing the system but Youtube would have to put actual effort into doing their damn job.

author: @cris_j
text: This is exactly the same with music reaction videos. Even though they drive more people to purchase/explore their content, generating more money for them, they still copyright strike everything out of some misguided attempt to exert 'control' over their content. This is why I recommend using ad blockers and pirating all of your media. If they can't take criticism, they don't deserve the income.

replies.author: @mardroidmk1393
replies.text:
Often with bands, it's not the band themselves but the record company raising the copywright issue. If you appeal to the folks themselves they'll often have the strike cancelled because they actually like the fact their stuff is being reviewed and therefore exposed to more people, thus expanding their fantom and purchases, etc. (Examples of this, Ren, Conquer Divide, Electric Callboy... the latter even do reactions to their reactions....😂. The others have been known to drop in thank you messages into comment sections, etc)

So, I would be careful about  pirating content.

replies.author: @cris_j
replies.text: @mardroidmk1393 It is still exactly the same problem, regardless WHO is doing it. There's an entity making it impossible for viewers to enjoy reaction content, because they hate anything they cannot control.

author: @platypusbuk
text:
AI auto flagging has struck again and again and again 

"Fair Use vs. Automation: The automated flagging does not always account for "fair use" (e.g., commentary, education, parody), which is a legal defense for using copyrighted material. The automated system simply identifies a match, and the user must then file a counter-notification to assert their fair use rights."

author: @sebastiaoferreiraramos1053
text: Lemons don't exist. The lemon is not a "natural" fruit in its pure state, but rather a hybrid created by humans in Asia, resulting from the crossbreeding of citron (Citrus medica) with bitter orange (Citrus aurantium). This process, which occurred centuries ago, resulted in the lemon we know today, a fruit with an acidic taste and intense aroma, which spread throughout the world through trade routes and migrations. In other words, if we have a problem, we create the solution.

author: @JoRoq1
text:
It’s more likely not the Forged in Fire production doing it directly.  Many companies hire outside social media management companies or legal firms to act as their agents.  These contractors regularly run general term searches and make mass copyright claims (regardless of how accurate or correct the claim is) to justify the exorbitant fees they charge and to take advantage of the weak design of YouTube’s strike and appeals processes.

I’ve heard of this happening to several other channels I follow.

author: @BreandanOCiarrai
text: As a business owner, malicious flagging on YT is a bad look, and any smart PR department would know that and tell the legal department to knock it the hell off. Flagging legit copyright infringement videos is one thing- and there are a lot of content creators out there who deserve it- but that's a far cry from flagging legit critiques, reviews, and reactions that clearly fall under Fair Use. Kneejerk reactionary flagging sours public opinion towards the company for no measurable fiscal gain, and possibly even fiscal loss. I know it's certainly made me less willing to waste my limited free time on Forged in Fire regardless of how good the show may or may not be, because their onerous abuse of YT's flagging system makes them seem like they are antagonistic towards their fanbase and I don't reward that with views.
